content_id:
Pembahasan al-Qasthallani tentang mukjizat-mukjizat kenabian (mu'jizat) mengikuti kerangka teologis Sunni yang telah mapan. Mukjizat didefinisikan sebagai tindakan luar biasa yang melanggar tatanan normal makhluk ciptaan, terwujud di tangan seorang nabi bersamaan dengan klaim kenabiannya, dengan cara yang menantang para penentang untuk menghasilkan yang serupa namun mereka tidak mampu. Berdasarkan definisi ini, mukjizat utama dan terbesar Nabi Muhammad adalah Al-Quran: sebuah teks berbahasa Arab dengan keunggulan sastra, intelektual, dan spiritual sedemikian rupa sehingga seluruh umat manusia dan jin tidak dapat menghasilkan karya yang sebanding. Al-Qasthallani membahas kemukjizatan (i'jaz) Al-Quran secara mendalam, menyebutkan bahwa para ulama telah mengidentifikasi berbagai dimensi kemukjizatan ini, termasuk keindahan bahasanya, konsistensi internalnya, kandungan prediktifnya yang tepat, dan kedalaman spiritualnya yang tak habis-habisnya yang terus menghasilkan makna sepanjang generasi.

Di luar Al-Quran, tradisi sirah menyimpan banyak catatan tentang peristiwa-peristiwa luar biasa yang berkaitan dengan Nabi. Al-Qasthallani mendekati hal ini dengan metodologi ahli hadis, membedakan antara yang memiliki sanad yang ketat dan yang bersifat pendukung. Di antara mukjizat material yang paling luas sahihnya adalah terbelahnya bulan (insyiqaq al-qamar), yang dibuktikan dalam Al-Quran itu sendiri (54:1-2) dan dikonfirmasi oleh riwayat-riwayat beberapa sahabat. Al-Qasthallani menyebutkan bahwa mukjizat ini terjadi secara terbuka, di hadapan orang-orang Quraisy yang menantang Nabi, dan bahwa rujukan Al-Quran terhadapnya mengkonfirmasinya melampaui keraguan apapun tentang rantai hadis.

Isra' dan Mi'raj diperlakukan sebagai mukjizat tingkat tertinggi sekaligus anugerah kedekatan spiritual yang secara unik bersifat kenabian. Tubuh Nabi dipindahkan dari Makkah ke Yerusalem dan kemudian naik menembus langit-langit dalam satu malam, dan beliau kembali sebelum fajar. Al-Qasthallani mendiskusikan posisi-posisi ulama mengenai apakah perjalanan itu bersifat jasmani atau spiritual, menyajikan pandangan mayoritas Sunni bahwa itu adalah perjalanan jasmani dalam keadaan terjaga. Beliau mencatat bahwa penggunaan Al-Quran atas kata 'abd (hamba) dalam merujuk pada Nabi selama Isra' menunjukkan kemanusiaan fisiknya sepenuhnya. Mukjizat-mukjizat yang berkaitan dengan Perjalanan tersebut, termasuk perjumpaan dengan para nabi di setiap langit, dicatat beserta rantai sanad dan komentar ilmiah.

Mukjizat-mukjizat sahih lainnya mencakup penggandaan air dan makanan untuk memenuhi kebutuhan komunitas pada saat-saat kritis. Di Hudaibiyah, para sahabat mendapati sebuah sumur hampir kering, dan anak panah Nabi diletakkan di dalamnya, setelah itu air mengalir dengan melimpah untuk seluruh rombongan yang berjumlah lebih dari seribu orang. Makanan dilipatgandakan dalam beberapa kesempatan, termasuk insiden terkenal Jabir ibn Abd Allah yang telah menyembelih seekor hewan kecil dan menyiapkan sejumlah roti yang sederhana, yang kemudian diberkati oleh Nabi dan mampu memberi makan hampir seribu orang. Al-Qasthallani menyajikan riwayat-riwayat ini sebagai bukti hubungan kenabian dengan kekuasaan ilahi, sambil mempertahankan kejelasan teologis bahwa mukjizat-mukjizat tersebut adalah tindakan Allah yang terwujud melalui Nabi, bukan kekuatan independen Nabi itu sendiri.
content_en:
Al-Qastallani's treatment of prophetic miracles (mu'jizat) follows the established Sunni theological framework. A miracle is defined as an extraordinary act that breaks the normal order of created things, performed at the hand of a prophet in conjunction with his prophetic claim, in a manner that challenges opponents to produce its like and that they cannot. By this definition, the primary and greatest miracle of the Prophet Muhammad is the Quran: an Arabic text of such literary, intellectual, and spiritual excellence that the entirety of humanity and jinn cannot produce a comparable work. Al-Qastallani discusses the inimitability (i'jaz) of the Quran in detail, noting that scholars have identified multiple dimensions of this inimitability, including its linguistic beauty, its internal consistency, its precise predictive content, and its inexhaustible spiritual depth that continues to yield meaning across generations.

Beyond the Quran, the Seerah tradition preserves numerous accounts of extraordinary events associated with the Prophet. Al-Qastallani approaches these with the methodology of the hadith scholar, distinguishing between those with rigorously authenticated chains and those that are supplementary. Among the most widely authenticated material miracles is the splitting of the moon (inshiqaq al-qamar), attested in the Quran itself (54:1-2) and confirmed by multiple Companion narrations. Al-Qastallani notes that this miracle was public, occurring in the presence of the Quraysh who challenged the Prophet, and that the Quran's reference to it confirms it beyond any possible doubt about the hadith chains.

The Night Journey and Ascension (Isra' wal-Mi'raj) are treated as both a miracle of the highest order and a gift of spiritual closeness that was uniquely prophetic. The Prophet's body was transported from Makkah to Jerusalem and then ascended through the heavens in a single night, and he returned before dawn. Al-Qastallani discusses the scholarly positions on whether the journey was bodily or spiritual, presenting the majority Sunni view that it was a bodily journey in the waking state. He notes that the Quran's use of the word 'abd (servant) in referring to the Prophet during the Night Journey indicates his full human physicality. The miracles associated with the Journey, including the encounter with the prophets in each heaven, are recorded with chains of transmission and scholarly commentary.

Other authenticated miracles include the multiplication of water and food to meet the needs of the community at critical moments. At Hudaybiyya, the Companions found a well nearly dry, and the Prophet's arrow was placed in it, after which the water flowed abundantly for the entire party of over a thousand people. Food was multiplied on several occasions, including the famous incident of Jabir ibn Abd Allah who had slaughtered a single small animal and prepared a modest amount of bread, which the Prophet blessed and which fed a gathering of nearly a thousand men. Al-Qastallani presents these narrations as evidence of the prophetic connection to divine power, while maintaining the theological clarity that the miracles were acts of God performed through the Prophet, not independent powers of the Prophet himself.
content_ar: Al-Qastallani's treatment of prophetic miracles (mu'jizat) follows the established Sunni theological framework. A miracle is defined as an extraordinary act that breaks the normal order of created things, performed at the hand of a النبي in conjunction with his prophetic claim, in a manner that challenges opponents to produce its like and that they cannot. By this definition, the الأساسي and greatest miracle of النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ is the القرآن الكريم: an Arabic text of such literary, intellectual, and spiritual excellence that the entirety of humanity and الجن cannot produce a comparable work. Al-Qastallani discusses the inimitability (i'jaz) of the القرآن الكريم in detail, noting that العلماء have identified multiple dimensions of this inimitability, بما في ذلك its linguistic beauty, its internal consistency, its precise predictive content, and its inexhaustible spiritual depth that continues to yield meaning across generations. Beyond the القرآن الكريم, the Seerah التراث preserves numerous accounts of extraordinary events associated with النبي ﷺ. Al-Qastallani approaches these with the methodology of the الحديث scholar, distinguishing between those with rigorously authenticated chains and those that are supplementary. Among the most widely authenticated material miracles is the splitting of the moon (inshiqaq al-qamar), attested in the القرآن الكريم itself (54:1-2) and confirmed by multiple Companion narrations. Al-Qastallani notes that this miracle was public, occurring in the presence of the Quraysh who challenged النبي ﷺ, and that the القرآن الكريم's reference to it confirms it beyond any possible doubt about the الحديث chains. The Night Journey and Ascension (Isra' wal-Mi'raj) are treated as both a miracle of the highest order and a الهبة of spiritual closeness that was uniquely prophetic. النبي ﷺ's الجسد was transported from مكة المكرمة to Jerusalem and then ascended through the heavens in a single night, and he returned قبل dawn. Al-Qastallani discusses the العلمي positions on whether the journey was bodily or spiritual, presenting the majority Sunni view that it was a bodily journey in the waking state. He notes that the القرآن الكريم's use of the word 'abd (servant) in referring to النبي ﷺ خلال the Night Journey indicates his full human physicality. The miracles associated with the Journey, بما في ذلك the encounter with the الأنبياء in each heaven, are recorded with chains of transmission and العلمي commentary. Other authenticated miracles include the multiplication of water and food to meet the needs of the الجماعة at البالغ الأهمية moments. At Hudaybiyya, the الصحابة found a well nearly dry, and النبي ﷺ's arrow was placed in it, بعد which the water flowed abundantly for the entire party of over a thousand people.
